title: 第 11 節
en: PRA&APATI, BBAHMAN, ATMAN. 73 in order to send forth, to animate^ and to rule all things. Whether this Prap&pati was himself the material cause of the world may seem doubtful. Many times it is said that he was everything and that he desired to become many, and thus created the world, in which case matter also would have come out of him. In other places, however, the primeval waters seem to have been admitted as existing by themselves and apart from Pra^apati (Bv. X, 1 2 1, 7). We also read that in the beginning there was water over which Pra#&pati breathed as wind and produced the earth, or that the waters themselves produced a golden egg from whence arose Pra^apati, the creator of gods and men. There occur even in the Brahmanas allusions to the legend well known from the Pur£/nas, that a boar brought forth (Udbabarha or Udvavarha from Vrih) the earth, or that a tortoise supported it1.

A belief in that Pra^&pati, as a personal god, was the beginning of monotheistic religion in India, while the recognition of Brahman and Atman, as one, constituted the foundation of all the monistic philosophy of that country.
CHAPTER III, THE SYSTEMS OF PHILOSOPHY. Growth of Philosophical Ideas.
WE have thus learnt the important lesson that all these ideas, metaphysical, cosmological, and otherwise, burst forth in India in great profusion and confusion, and -without any ^preconceived system.

We must not suppose that these ideas follow each other in chronological succession. Here once more the Nebeneinander gives us the true key, much more than the Nackeinander. We must remember that this earliest philosophy existed for a long time without being fixed by writing, that there was neither control, authority, nor public opinion to protect it. Every Asrama or settlement was a world by itself, even the simplest means of communication, such as high-roads or rivers, being often wanting. The wonder is that, in spite of all this, we should find so much unity in the numerous guesses at truth preserved to us among these Vedic ruins. This -was due, we are told, to the Parampara, i. e. to those who handed down 'the tradition and at la'st collected whatever could be saved of it. It would be a mistake to imagine that there "was a continuous development in the various meanings assumed by or assigned to such pregnant terms as Pra^^pati, Brahman, or even Atman. It is much more in accordance with what we learn from the Br^hma^as and TJpanishads of the intellectual life of India, to admit an infinite number of intellectual centres of thought, scattered all over the country, in which either the one or the other view found influential advocates. We should then understand better PRASTHA.NA BHEDA. 75 how Brahman, while meaning what bursts or drives forth, came to signify speech andx prayer, as well as creative power aird creator, and why Atman meant not only breath, tutlife, spirit, soul, essence, or what I have ventured to reader ly the Self, das Selbst, of all things.

But if in the period of the Brahma^as and Upanishads \ve listve to find our way through religious and philosophical Ihoughts, as through clusters of thickly tangled creepers, the outlook becomes brighter as soon as we approach, the next period, which is characterised by persistent attempts at clear and systematic thought. We must noi> imagine that even then we can always discover in the rarious systems of philosophy a regular historical growth, The Sfttras or aphorisms which we possess of the six systems of philosophy, each distinct from the other, cannot possibly claim to represent the very first attempts at a systematic treatment ; they are rather the last summing tip of %vh.^t had been growing up during many generations of isolated thinkers.
Frastli&na Bfceda.

^ What ihe Brahmans themselves thought of their philosophical literature we may learn even from such modern treatises a,s the Prasthana-bheda, from which I gave some extracts "fcy way of introduction to some papers of mine on one of th<e systems of Indian philosophy, published as long ago as i&5a in the Journal of the German Oriental Society. It is but fair to state that the credit of having discovered that tract of Madhusudana Sarasvatt, and perceived its Importance, belonged really to Colebrooke. I myself came to be acquainted with it through my old friend, Dr. Trithen, \rtio had prepared a critical edition of it, but was prevented "by Illness, and death from publishing it. It was published in the meantime by Professor Weber in his Indische Studien, 184.9, spd ^ think it may be useful to give once more some extracts fcom it 1.
1 -A new translation of the Prasthana-bheda has been published by Prof. I>eus.sen as an Introduction to his Allgemeine Geschichte der , vol. i, p. 44, 1894.

f Nyaya Y he writes, ' is logic2, as promulgated by Gotarna3 in five Adhyayas (lessons). Its object is knowledge of the nature of the sixteen Pad£rthas by means of name, definition, and examination/ These Padarthas are the important or essential topics of the Nyaya philosophy; but it has proved very misleading to see Pad&rtha here translated by categories. No one could understand why such things as doubt, example, wrangling, &c., could possibly be called categories or praedicaJbilia, and it is no wonder that Eitter and others should have spoken of the Ny&ya with open contempt, as they have done, if such things were represented to them as the categories of Indian logic.
f There is also the Vaiseshika philosophy in ten lessons, promulgated by Ka?i&da. Its object is to establish by their similarities and dissimilarities 4 the six Padarthas, viz. : —
1. Dravya, substance.
2. Guwa, quality.
3. Karinan, activity.
4. Samanya, what is general and found in more than one object. The highest Samanya is Satta or being.
5. Visesha, the differentia or what is special, residing in eternal atoms, &c.
6. Samavaya, inseparable inherence, as between cause and effect, parts and the whole, &c.
To which may be added 7. Abhava, negation.

This philosophy also is called Nyaya/ These Padarthas of the Vaiseshikas, at least 1-5, may indeed be called categories, for they represent what can be 1 Nyaya is derived from ni 4 into/ and i * to go.' The fourth member of a syllogism is called Upanaya, * leading towards* or t induction/ Ballantyne translates Nyaya by yn0oSos-.

3 Anvikshiki as an old name of philosophy, more particularly of logic, occurs also in Gautama's Dharmasa&tra II, 3. It is used sometimes as synonymous with Mimamsa; and is more comprehensive than logic.
3 As the MSS. vary between Gotama and Gautama, I have kept the former for the Nyaya, 'philosopher,' the latter for Buddha.

4 Barthelemy St. Hilaire, in his work on Indian Logic, p. 356, remarks, categories entre elles, en e"numerant leurs proprie"te*s, comme Ta fait le Stagirite. II n'a point montre", eomme Aristote, leurs rapports et leurs differences/ But this is exactly what he has done, of, Sutras I, 8 seq.

PRASTEANA BEEDA. 77 predicated, in general, of the objects of our experience, or, from an Indian point of view, what is predicated by, or what is the highest sense (Artha) of words (Pada). Thus it has come to pass that Padartha, literally the meaning of a Arord, was used in Sanskrit in the sense of things in general, or objects. It is rightly translated by category when applied to the five Padarthas of Ka^ada, but such a translation, doubtful even in the case of the sixth or seventh Padartha of the Vaiseshikas, would of course be quite misleading when applied to the Padarthas of Gotama. The real categories would, in Gotama's system, find their place mostly under Prameya, meaning not so much what has to be proved or established, as what forms the object of our knowledge.

Madhusftdana continues : e The Mimamsa also is twof old, viz. the Karma-Mimams& (work-philosophy) and the £&riraka-Mimamsa (philosophy of the embodied spirit). The Karma-Mimamsa has been brought out by the venerable C?aimini in twelve chapters/ The objects of these twelve chapters are then indicated very shortly, and so as to be hardly intelligible without a reference to the original Sutras. Dharma, the object of this philosophy, is explained as consisting of acts of duty, chiefly sacrificial. The second, third, and fourth chapters treat 1 of the differences and varieties of Dharma, its parts (or appendent members, contrasted with the main act), and the principal purpose of each sacrificial performance. The fifth chapter tries to settle the order of all sacrificial performances, and the sixth the qualifications of its performers. The subject of indirect precepts is opened in the seventh chapter and carried on more fully in the eighth. Inferrible changes, adapting to any variation or copy of certain sacrificial acts what was designed for the types or models of them, are discussed in the ninth, and bars or exceptions in the tenth. Concurrent efficacy is considered in the eleventh chapter, and co-ordinate effect in the twelfth ; that is, the co-operation of several acts for a single result is the I give this more intelligible Description from Colebrooke, Miscellaneous , voj, i, p. 33° seq., 78 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
subject of the one, and the incidental effect of an act, of which the chief purpose is different, is discussed in the other l.

'There is also the SamkarshaMa-ka^a, consisting of four chapters, composed by ffaimini, and this, which is known by the name of Devata-kancZa, belongs to the Karma-Mimawsa, because it teaches the act called Upasana or worship.

' Next follows the $ariraka-Mima?ttsa, consisting of four chapters. Its^ object is to make clear the oneness of Brahman and Atman (Self), and to exhibit the rules which teach the investigation (of it) by means of Yedic study, &c.' It is in fact much more what we call a system of philosophy than the Purva-Mimamsa, and it is quoted by different names, such as Uttara-Mimamsa, Brahma-Mimamsa, Vedanta, &c. 2 ' In the first lecture is shown the agreement with which all Vedanta passages refer, directly or indirectly, to the inward, undivided, second-less Brahman. In the first section are considered Vedic passages which have clear indications of Brahman ; in the second, passages which have obscure indications of Brahman, and refer to Brahman so far as he is an object of worship; in the third, passages which have obscure indications of Brahman, and mostly refer to Brahman, so far as he or it is an object of knowledge. Thus the consideration of the Vedanta texts has been finished, and in the fourth section such words as Avyakta, At/a, &c.5 are considered, of which it can be doubtful whether they may not refer to ideas, adapted and formulated by the Sawkhya philosophers, such as Pradhana, Prakriti, which is generally, though quite wrongly, translated by nature, as independent of Brahman or Purusha.

' The convergence of all Vedanta texts on the second-less Brahman having thus been established, Vyasa or Badaraa, fearing an opposition by means of arguments such as ^ 1 Professor ^Deussen has given a somewhat different version of these titles. He gives, for instance, as the subject of the fifth chapter the successive order of recitation, as enjoined by Sriiti, but to judge from Mim. Sutras V, i, i, the right meaning seems to be the 'settling of the order of performance, according to Sruti, subject-matter, recitation. &c.' 2 Read Adya for Akhya in the Frasthana bheda, PRASTHANA BEEDA. 79 have been produced by acknowledged Smritis and various other systems, undertakes their refutation, and tries to establish the incontrovertible validity of his own arguments in the second lecture. Here, in the first section, the objections to. the convergence of the Vedanta passages on Brahman, as stated by the Smritis of the Samkhya-yoga, the Kanadas, and by the arguments employed by the S&mkhyas, are disposed of. In the second section is shown the faultiness of the views of the followers of the Samkhya, because every examination should consist of two parts, the establishment of our own doctrine and the refutation of the doctrine of our opponents. In the third section the contradictions between the passages of the Veda, referring to the creation of the elements and other subjects, are removed in the first part, and in the second those referring to individual souls. In the fourth section are considered all apparent contradictions between Vedic passages referring to the senses and their objects.

fln the third chapter follows the examination of the means (of salvation). Here in the first section, while considering the going to and returning from another world (transmigration), dispassionateness has to be examined. In the second section the meaning of the word Thou is made clear, and afterwards the meaning of the word That. In the third section there is a collection of words, if not purely tautological, all referring to the unqualified Brahman, as recorded in different S&kMs or branches of the Yeda ; and at the same time the question is discussed whether certain attributes' recorded by other $akMs in teaching a qualified or unqualified Brahman, may be taken together or not. In the fourth section the means of obtaining a knowledge of the unqualified Brahman, both the external, such as sacrifices and observing the four stations in life, and the internal, such as quietness, control, and meditation, are investigated.
zh: 普拉賈帕蒂（Prapāpati）為了派遣、賦予生命力並統治萬物而存在。這個普拉賈帕蒂本身是否為世界的物質因，似乎令人懷疑。許多地方說他就是一切，且渴望成為眾多，因此創造了世界，在此情況下物質也必然來自於他。然而在其他地方，原初的水似乎被承認為獨立存在，與普拉賈帕蒂分離（梵書 X, 1 2 1, 7）。我們也讀到，在太初之時有水覆蓋大地，普拉賈帕蒂如風般對其吹氣並產生了陸地，或者那些水本身產生了一枚金蛋，從中出生了普拉賈帕蒂，諸神與人類的創造者。甚至在《梵書》中也出現了從《往世書》（Purāṇas）所熟知的傳說的隱喻，即一頭野豬產生了大地（Udbabarha 或 Udvavarha，源自 Vrih），或者一隻烏龜支撐了它。
對作為人格神的原主神（Pra^&pati）的信仰，乃是印度一神教宗教的開端，而將梵天（Brahman）與自我（Atman）視為一體的認識，則構成了該國所有一元論哲學的基礎。
第三章　哲學的體系　哲學觀念的發展
因此，我們學到了一個重要的教訓：所有這些理念，無論是形而上學的、宇宙論的或其他性質的，都在印度蓬勃迸發，紛亂繁雜，並且並未遵循任何預先構想的體系。

我們不應該假設這些觀念按時間先後順序相繼出現。在此，「並存」（Nebeneinander）再次為我們提供了真正的鑰匙，遠勝於「依次」（Nackeinander）。我們必須記住，這種最早的哲學存在了很長時間而未被文字固定，沒有任何控制、權威或輿論來保護它。每個苦行林（Asrama）或定居點都是自成一體的世界，即使是最簡單的交通工具，如高速公路或河流，往往也付之闕如。令人驚嘆的是，儘管有所有這些困難，我們仍在保存於這些吠陀遺跡中的眾多真理臆測中發現了如此多的統一性。據說，這歸功於傳承法（Parampara），即那些傳承傳統並最終蒐集了能夠保存之物者。想像存在著對於梵天祭主（Prajāpati）、梵（Brahman）甚至自我（Ātman）這類含義深遠的術語所具有或被賦予的各種涵義之連續發展，這將是一個錯誤。更加符合我們從梵書（Brāhmana）和奧義書（Upanishad）所瞭解的印度知識生活的情況的做法，是承認存在著無限數量的思想知識中心，散佈於整個國家各地，在這些中心中，這樣或那樣的觀點各有其有影響力的倡導者。這樣我們就能更好地理解為何梵（Brahman）在本義為「迸發或驅動」時，卻來具有了語言和祈禱、以及創造力與造物者之意義，以及為何自我（Ātman）不僅表示呼吸，而且表示生命、精神、靈魂、本質，或我大膽稱之為萬物之自我（das Selbst）。

但是，如果在梵書（Brahmaṇas）與奧義書的時期，我們要在宗教與哲學思想中找尋我們的道路，如同穿過密集纏繞的藤蔓叢，那麼一旦我們接近下一個時期，展望就變得更為光明了。該時期的特色在於對清晰而系統化思想的持續努力。我們不能想像即便在當時，我們也總能在各種哲學體系中發現有規律的歷史發展。我們所擁有的六大哲學體系的蘇特羅（Sūtras，格言）—— 每一個都各具特色 —— 不可能聲稱代表了系統性論述的最初嘗試；它們毋寧說是在許多代孤立的思想家期間累積發展而來之物的最後總結。
我注意到您提供的「英文原文」（Frastli&na Bfceda）似乎是損壞或誤掃的文字，無法辨識為有意義的英文內容。 請您重新提供清晰的英文原文，我將按照您的規則進行專業的繁體中文翻譯。

婆羅門人自己對其哲學文獻的看法，我們甚至可以從《論派區分》（Prasthana-bheda）這樣的現代論著中得知，我曾從中摘錄某些段落，作為我發表於一八五〇年《德國東方學會期刊》上關於印度哲學體系之一的數篇論文的導論。應當公允地說，發現馬度蘇達那‧薩拉斯瓦蒂的論著並認識到其重要性的功勞實際上屬於科爾布魯克。我本人是通過我的老友特里森博士認識它的，他曾準備了該著作的批判性版本，但因疾病與去世而未能出版。與此同時，韋伯教授在其《印度研究》（Indische Studien）中於一八四九年出版了它，我認為再次摘錄其中某些段落可能會有用處。
1 —普拉斯塔那‧貝達（Prasthana-bheda）的新譯本已由德烏森教授出版，作為其《宗教通史》第一卷第44頁《導論》之一部分，1894年。

論及尼耶耶哲學，他寫道：「尼耶耶是邏輯學，乃由喬達摩（Gotama）以五部論著（Adhyayas）的形式所闡述。其目的在於通過名稱、定義和考察的方式，獲得對十六個帕達爾塔（Padarthas）之本質的知識。」這些帕達爾塔是尼耶耶哲學中的重要或本質主題；然而，將帕達爾塔在此譯為「範疇」已被證明極具誤導性。沒有人能夠理解為何疑惑、例證、詭辯等事物竟可能被稱為範疇或述語，如果這些事物被呈現為印度邏輯的範疇，里特（Ritter）及其他人對尼耶耶開放地表達輕蔑，確實不足為奇。
還有由迦尼亞達（Kaṇāda）所頒布的十篇課程構成的瓦伊舍希卡（Vaiseshika）哲學。其目標是通過相似性與差異性來確立六種帕達爾塔（Padarthas），即：——
德拉維亞（Dravya），實體。
2. Guwa，品質。
3. 卡利南（Karinan），活動。
4. 薩曼雅（Samanya），即存在於一個以上對象中的普遍性。最高的薩曼雅是薩塔（Satta）或存在（being）。
5. 毗舍沙（Visesha），即區別性或特殊性，存於永恆的原子中等。
6. 薩摩耶（Samavaya），不可分離的內在性，如原因與結果、部分與整體等之間的關係。
可再加上第七項：Abhava，即否定。

這個哲學派系也被稱為尼耶耶（Nyaya）。毘世師派（Vaiseshikas）的這些句義論（Padarthas），至少前五類，確實可以稱為範疇，因為它們代表了……¹ ¹ 尼耶耶（Nyaya）乃衍生自「尼」（ni）意為「進入」與「耶」（i）意為「去」。三段論的第四個部分稱為「上納耶」（Upanaya），意為「導向」或「歸納」。巴蘭泰因（Ballantyne）將尼耶耶（Nyaya）譯為「ἐπιστήμη」。
3 Anvikshiki 作為哲學（尤其是邏輯學）的古稱，也出現在瞿曇（Gautama）的《法經》（Dharmasastra）第二卷第三節中。它有時被用作與彌曼差（Mimamsa）同義；其涵蓋範圍更為廣泛，超越邏輯學本身。
由於古寫本在 Gotama 與 Gautama 之間有所差異，我在尼耶耶（Nyaya）「哲學家」一詞中保留前者，而在佛陀一詞中採用後者。
巴特雷米‧聖‧伊萊爾在其《印度邏輯》一書第356頁評論道，各範疇之間的性質，如同古人所做的那樣，經由列舉它們的特性而得以表述。他並未如亞里斯多德那樣展示其關係與差異。然而這正是他在《蘇特羅經》第一卷第8段及其後所做之事。

普拉斯蒂納‧必達。77 一般而言是述說我們經驗的對象，或從印度的觀點看，是由什麼所述說，或者說是詞語（Pada）最高的意義（Artha）。因此便出現了這樣的情況：帕達爾塔（Padartha）字面上意為詞語的意義，在梵文中被用來表示事物一般或對象。當應用於迦葉多（Kaṇāda）的五種帕達爾塔時，將其譯為範疇（category）是恰當的，但這種翻譯即使在毗舍耶迦派（Vaiseshikas）的第六或第七帕達爾塔的情況下就已有其可疑之處，當應用於高塔馬（Gotama）的帕達爾塔時，當然就完全會導人誤途了。在高塔馬的體系中，實際的範疇應當主要歸屬於普拉梅亞（Prameya），其意義與其說是指必須被證明或建立的事物，不如說是指構成我們知識對象的事物。

馬德胡蘇丹那繼續說道：「彌曼差哲學也是雙重的，即業彌曼差（行為哲學）與沙理拉卡彌曼差（具身精神的哲學）。業彌曼差由尊崇的者依彌尼以十二章的形式闡述。這十二章的內容隨後被很簡要地指示出來，以至於若不參照原始經文，幾乎無法理解。此哲學的對象——法（dharma）——被解釋為包含行為義務，主要是祭祀性的。第二、三、四章論述法的區分與種類、其部分（或附屬成分，與主要行為相對照）以及各祭祀儀式的主要目的。第五章試圖確立所有祭祀儀式的順序，第六章確立其施行者的資格。第七章開啟間接誡命的主題，並在第八章更充分地展開。在第九章討論可推導出的變化——將為類型或典範所設計的內容調整適用於其任何變異或副本——而在第十章討論禁止或例外。第十一章考慮並行的效力，第十二章考慮協調的結果；即，數種行為為了單一結果的合作乃是……
一篇文獻中討論某一主體，而另一篇文獻則討論某一行為的附帶效果，儘管該行為的主要目的不同。
還有《沙摩卡沙》（SamkarshaMa-ka^a），由智慧仙人（Jaimini）所作，分為四章，此書以《提婆塔堪查》（Devata-kancZa）之名而聞，屬於業論祭祀學（Karma-Mimawsa），因為它教導稱為禮拜（Upasana）或崇拜的儀式行為。

接著是《莎麗剎卡彌馬姆薩》（Śarīraka-Mimāṁsā），由四章組成。其目的在於闡明梵天（Brahman）與自我（Ātman）的一體性，並展示通過吠陀研究等方式進行其調查研究的規則。實際上，它比《普爾瓦彌馬姆薩》更接近我們所謂的哲學體系，並以不同的名稱被引用，例如《烏脫剌彌馬姆薩》（Uttara-Mimāṁsā）、《梵天彌馬姆薩》（Brahma-Mimāṁsā）、《韋檀多》（Vedānta）等。在第一講中，展示了所有韋檀多經文以何種一致性直接或間接地指向內在的、不分割的、無二的梵天。在第一節考察了對梵天有明確指示的吠陀經文；在第二節，考察了對梵天有模糊指示的經文，並在梵天作為崇拜對象的範圍內指向梵天；在第三節，考察了對梵天有模糊指示的經文，並大多在梵天作為知識對象的範圍內指向梵天。如此，對韋檀多經文的考察已經完成，在第四節考察了《阿毗亞克塔》（Avyakta）、《阿特瑪》（Ātman）等詞彙，對於這些詞彙是否可能指向由數論（Sāṁkhya）哲學家所適應與闡述的觀念（如本初質性〔Pradhāna〕、物質〔Prakṛti〕——這通常被誤譯為「自然」）而與梵天或人我（Purusha）無關，是可以懷疑的。

吠檀多諸文本對於無二之梵天（Brahman）的匯聚既已確立，毗耶娑或跋達羅遂恐懼將遭受由公認之《法經》（Smritis）與各種其他體系所提出之論證的反對，乃著手駁斥之，並於第二講中試圖確立其自身論證之不可推翻之有效性。於此，在第一節中，駁斥對於吠檀多經文對梵天之匯聚的異議，此等異議係由數論瑜伽派之《法經》、卡那多派（Kanadas）及數論派所採用之論證所提出。在第二節中，揭示數論派追隨者諸見之謬誤，蓋每一檢驗應由兩部分組成，即確立我等自身之教說與駁斥吾人對手之教說。在第三節中，吠陀諸經文間關於元素之創造及其他主題之矛盾，於其第一部分予以消除，於其第二部分則消除關於個別靈魂之矛盾。在第四節中，考量吠陀諸經文間關於感根及其對象所有表面上之矛盾。 --- *註：原文中 ^ 1 標記及其對應腳注內容（Professor Deussen...）係學術申注，保留原貌，不另行翻譯或重組。*

在第三章中，接著檢視救贖的手段。在第一節中，當考察往來於另一世界（輪迴）之際，必須檢視無欲。在第二節中，闡明「汝」（Thou）一詞的意義，隨後闡明「彼」（That）一詞的意義。在第三節中，集合各種詞彙，如果不純粹是重言反覆的話，所有這些詞彙都涉及無限定的梵天（unqualified Brahman），如梵天經各支派（Śākhās）或吠陀各分科中所記載；與此同時，討論了其他支派在教導有限定的或無限定的梵天時所記載的某些屬性，是否可以合併考慮的問題。在第四節中，調查了獲得無限定梵天之知識的手段，既包括外在的手段，如祭祀和遵守人生四個階段，也包括內在的手段，如寧靜、克制和冥想。
